extracted_information: Docker user namespace remapping is a security feature that isolates containers by mapping the `root` user inside a container to an unprivileged user on the Docker host. This prevents privilege-escalation attacks and enhances security by ensuring that even if a process within a container runs as `root`, its corresponding host UID/GID has no privileges on the host machine. This technique is a key component in achieving a 'rootless' security posture for containers.

example_mapping.details: testuser is assigned a subordinate UID range starting at 231072 for 65536 integers. Host UID 231072 maps to container UID 0 (root), 231073 to UID 1, and so forth.

specifications.kernel_limitation: Docker uses a maximum of 5 non-overlapping mappings per user/group due to kernel limitation for `/proc/self/uid_map` and `/proc/self/gid_map` entries.

features.name: Security Isolation (UID/GID remapping)
features.description: Remaps the container's `root` user to a high-numbered, unprivileged UID on the host, preventing privilege escalation from within the container. Processes have no privileges on the host system.

features.name: Transparency to Container
features.description: The re-mapping is transparent to the container, which perceives itself as running as `root` or other UIDs.

features.name: Default User Creation
features.description: If 'default' is specified, Docker creates a user and group named `dockremap` for remapping purposes.

features.name: Prerequisites for Enabling
details: Subordinate UID/GID ranges must be associated with an existing user (or Docker can create `dockremap`).
User owns namespaced storage directories under `/var/lib/docker/`.
Ensure `/etc/subuid` and `/etc/subgid` contain correct, non-overlapping entries (manual editing may be required on some distributions).
Adjust host permissions for bind mounts if the remapped user needs write access.
Ranges must not overlap to prevent namespace access.
Existing image/container layers are masked; best enabled on a new Docker installation.

features.name: Directory Restructuring
features.description: Enabling `userns-remap` results in Docker storing container data in a subdirectory under `/var/lib/docker/` named with the remapped UID and GID (e.g., `/var/lib/docker/231072.231072/`), owned by the remapped user.

features.name: Disabling for Specific Container
features.commands: `docker container create`, `docker container run`, `docker container exec`
features.side_effect: Read-only (image) layers remain remapped, leading to container filesystem ownership being remapped to the daemon's remapped user. Can cause issues with `sudo` or `setuid` binaries inside the container.

features.name: Known Limitations
incompatibilities: Sharing PID or NET namespaces with the host (`--pid=host` or `--network=host`).
External (volume or storage) drivers not supporting daemon user mappings.
other_limitations: Requires coordination for host volume mounts regarding file ownership.
Container `root` user cannot use `mknod` command (device creation denied).

temporal_info.enabling_recommendation: Best to enable `userns-remap` on a new Docker installation rather than an existing one.
temporal_info.disabling_impact: Resources created while `userns-remap` was enabled become inaccessible if the feature is later disabled.
